````text
This is a [Next.js](https://nextjs.org) project bootstrapped with [`create-next-app`](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/cli/create-next-app).

## Getting Started

First, run the development server:

```bash
npm run dev
# or
yarn dev
# or
pnpm dev
# or
bun dev
```

Open [http://localhost:3000](http://localhost:3000) with your browser to see the result.

You can start editing the page by modifying `app/page.tsx`. The page auto-updates as you edit the file.

This project uses [`next/font`](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/optimizing/fonts) to automatically optimize and load [Geist](https://vercel.com/font), a new font family for Vercel.

## Learn More

To learn more about Next.js, take a look at the following resources:

- [Next.js Documentation](https://nextjs.org/docs) - learn about Next.js features and API.
- [Learn Next.js](https://nextjs.org/learn) - an interactive Next.js tutorial.

You can check out [the Next.js GitHub repository](https://github.com/vercel/next.js) - your feedback and contributions are welcome!

## Deploy on Vercel

The easiest way to deploy your Next.js app is to use the [Vercel Platform](https://vercel.com/new?utm_medium=default-template&filter=next.js&utm_source=create-next-app&utm_campaign=create-next-app-readme) from the creators of Next.js.
````

node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app/03-api-reference/05-config/01-next-config-js/output.md:80:- There are some cases in which Next.js might fail to include required files, or might incorrectly include unused files. In those cases, you can leverage `outputFileTracingExcludes` and `outputFileTracingIncludes` respectively in `next.config.js`. Each option accepts an object whose keys are **route globs** (matched with [picomatch](https://www.npmjs.com/package/picomatch#basic-globbing) against the route path, e.g. `/api/hello`) and whose values are **glob patterns resolved from the project root** that specify files to include or exclude in the trace.

### Assistant [commentary] (2026-05-19T16:35:41.679Z)

The local Next docs confirm this matters in monorepos: the folder containing `next.config.*` is the Next project root, and ESLint can point at app roots when Next is not at repository root. I’m going to keep the repo root as a pnpm workspace and make `frontend/` the Next project root.

````text
---
title: ESLint Plugin
nav_title: ESLint
description: Learn how to use and configure the ESLint plugin to catch common issues and problems in a Next.js application.
---

{/* The content of this doc is shared between the app and pages router. You can use the `<PagesOnly>Content</PagesOnly>` component to add content that is specific to the Pages Router. Any shared content should not be wrapped in a component. */}

Next.js provides an ESLint configuration package, [`eslint-config-next`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/eslint-config-next), that makes it easy to catch common issues in your application. It includes the [`@next/eslint-plugin-next`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@next/eslint-plugin-next) plugin along with recommended rule-sets from [`eslint-plugin-react`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/eslint-plugin-react) and [`eslint-plugin-react-hooks`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/eslint-plugin-react-hooks).

The package provides two main configurations:

- **`eslint-config-next`**: Base configuration with Next.js, React, and React Hooks rules. Supports both JavaScript and TypeScript files.
- **`eslint-config-next/core-web-vitals`**: Includes everything from the base config, plus upgrades rules that impact [Core Web Vitals](https://web.dev/vitals/) from warnings to errors. Recommended for most projects.

Additionally, for TypeScript projects:

- **`eslint-config-next/typescript`**: Adds TypeScript-specific linting rules from [`typescript-eslint`](https://typescript-eslint.io/). Use this alongside the base or core-web-vitals config.

## Setup ESLint

Get linting working quickly with the ESLint CLI (flat config):

1. Install ESLint and the Next.js config:

   ```bash package="pnpm"
   pnpm add -D eslint eslint-config-next
   ```

   ```bash package="npm"
   npm i -D eslint eslint-config-next
   ```

   ```bash package="yarn"
   yarn add --dev eslint eslint-config-next
   ```

   ```bash package="bun"
   bun add -d eslint eslint-config-next
   ```

2. Create `eslint.config.mjs` with the Next.js config:

   ```js filename="eslint.config.mjs"
   import { defineConfig, globalIgnores } from 'eslint/config'
   import nextVitals from 'eslint-config-next/core-web-vitals'

   const eslintConfig = defineConfig([
     ...nextVitals,
     // Override default ignores of eslint-config-next.
     globalIgnores([
       // Default ignores of eslint-config-next:
       '.next/**',
       'out/**',
       'build/**',
       'next-env.d.ts',
     ]),
   ])

   export default eslintConfig
   ```

3. Run ESLint:

   ```bash package="pnpm"
   pnpm exec eslint .
   ```

   ```bash package="npm"
   npx eslint .
   ```

   ```bash package="yarn"
   yarn eslint .
   ```

   ```bash package="bun"
   bunx eslint .
   ```

## Reference

The `eslint-config-next` package includes the `recommended` rule-sets from the following ESLint plugins:

- [`eslint-plugin-react`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/eslint-plugin-react)
- [`eslint-plugin-react-hooks`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/eslint-plugin-react-hooks)
- [`@next/eslint-plugin-next`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@next/eslint-plugin-next)

### Rules

The `@next/eslint-plugin-next` rules included are:

| Enabled in recommended config | Rule                                                                                                                     | Description                                                                                                      |
| :---------------------------: | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
|      <Check size={18} />      | [@next/next/google-font-display](/docs/messages/google-font-display)                                                     | Enforce font-display behavior with Google Fonts.                                                                 |
|      <Check size={18} />      | [@next/next/google-font-preconnect](/docs/messages/google-font-preconnect)                                               | Ensure `preconnect` is used with Google Fonts.                                                                   |
|      <Check size={18} />      | [@next/next/inline-script-id](/docs/messages/inline-script-id)                                                           | Enforce `id` attribute on `next/script` components with inline content.                                          |
|      <Check size={18} />      | [@next/next/next-script-for-ga](/docs/messages/next-script-for-ga)                                                       | Prefer `next/script` component when using the inline script for Google Analytics.                                |
|      <Check size={18} />      | [@next/next/no-assign-module-variable](/docs/messages/no-assign-module-variable)                                         | Prevent assignment to the `module` variable.                                                                     |
|      <Check size={18} />      | [@next/next/no-async-client-component](/docs/messages/no-async-client-component)                                         | Prevent Client Components from being async functions.                                                            |
|      <Check size={18} />      | [@next/next/no-before-interactive-script-outside-document](/docs/messages/no-before-interactive-script-outside-document) | Prevent usage of `next/script`'s `beforeInteractive` strategy outside of `pages/_document.js`.                   |
|      <Check size={18} />      | [@next/next/no-css-tags](/docs/messages/no-css-tags)                                                                     | Prevent manual stylesheet tags.                                                                                  |
|      <Check size={18} />      | [@next/next/no-document-import-in-page](/docs/messages/no-document-import-in-page)                                       | Prevent importing `next/document` outside of `pages/_document.js`.                                               |
|      <Check size={18} />      | [@next/next/no-duplicate-head](/docs/messages/no-duplicate-head)                                                         | Prevent duplicate usage of `<Head>` in `pages/_document.js`.                                                     |
|      <Check size={18} />      | [@next/next/no-head-element](/docs/messages/no-head-element)                                                             | Prevent usage of `<head>` element.                                                                               |
|      <Check size={18} />      | [@next/next/no-head-import-in-document](/docs/messages/no-head-import-in-document)                                       | Prevent usage of `next/head` in `pages/_document.js`.                                                            |
|      <Check size={18} />      | [@next/next/no-html-link-for-pages](/docs/messages/no-html-link-for-pages)                                               | Prevent usage of `<a>` elements to navigate to internal Next.js pages.                                           |
|      <Check size={18} />      | [@next/next/no-img-element](/docs/messages/no-img-element)                                                               | Prevent usage of `<img>` element due to slower LCP and higher bandwidth.                                         |
|      <Check size={18} />      | [@next/next/no-page-custom-font](/docs/messages/no-page-custom-font)                                                     | Prevent page-only custom fonts.                                                                                  |
|      <Check size={18} />      | [@next/next/no-script-component-in-head](/docs/messages/no-script-component-in-head)                                     | Prevent usage of `next/script` in `next/head` component.                                                         |
|      <Check size={18} />      | [@next/next/no-styled-jsx-in-document](/docs/messages/no-styled-jsx-in-document)                                         | Prevent usage of `styled-jsx` in `pages/_document.js`.                                                           |
|      <Check size={18} />      | [@next/next/no-sync-scripts](/docs/messages/no-sync-scripts)                                                             | Prevent synchronous scripts.                                                                                     |
|      <Check size={18} />      | [@next/next/no-title-in-document-head](/docs/messages/no-title-in-document-head)                                         | Prevent usage of `<title>` with `Head` component from `next/document`.                                           |
|      <Check size={18} />      | @next/next/no-typos                                                                                                      | Prevent common typos in [Next.js's data fetching functions](/docs/pages/building-your-application/data-fetching) |
|      <Check size={18} />      | [@next/next/no-unwanted-polyfillio](/docs/messages/no-unwanted-polyfillio)                                               | Prevent duplicate polyfills from Polyfill.io.                                                                    |

We recommend using an appropriate [integration](https://eslint.org/docs/user-guide/integrations#editors) to view warnings and errors directly in your code editor during development.

<details>
  <summary>`next lint` removal</summary>

Starting with Next.js 16, `next lint` is removed.

As part of the removal, the `eslint` option in your Next config file is no longer needed and can be safely removed.

</details>

## Examples

### Specifying a root directory within a monorepo

If you're using `@next/eslint-plugin-next` in a project where Next.js isn't installed in your root directory (such as a monorepo), you can tell `@next/eslint-plugin-next` where to find your Next.js application using the `settings` property in your `eslint.config.mjs`:

```js filename="eslint.config.mjs"
import { defineConfig } from 'eslint/config'
import eslintNextPlugin from '@next/eslint-plugin-next'

const eslintConfig = defineConfig([
  {
    files: ['**/*.{js,jsx,ts,tsx}'],
    plugins: {
      next: eslintNextPlugin,
    },
    settings: {
      next: {
        rootDir: 'packages/my-app/',
      },
    },
  },
])

export default eslintConfig
```

`rootDir` can be a path (relative or absolute), a glob (i.e. `"packages/*/"`), or an array of paths and/or globs.

### Disabling rules

If you would like to modify or disable any rules provided by the supported plugins (`react`, `react-hooks`, `next`), you can directly change them using the `rules` property in your `eslint.config.mjs`:

```js filename="eslint.config.mjs"
import { defineConfig, globalIgnores } from 'eslint/config'
import nextVitals from 'eslint-config-next/core-web-vitals'

const eslintConfig = defineConfig([
  ...nextVitals,
  {
    rules: {
      'react/no-unescaped-entities': 'off',
      '@next/next/no-page-custom-font': 'off',
    },
  },
  // Override default ignores of eslint-config-next.
  globalIgnores([
    // Default ignores of eslint-config-next:
    '.next/**',
    'out/**',
    'build/**',
    'next-env.d.ts',
  ]),

````

````text
Output:
> **Good to know**:
>
> - `next.config.js` is read during `next build` and serialized into the `server.js` output file.
> - If your project needs to listen to a specific port or hostname, you can define `PORT` or `HOSTNAME` environment variables before running `server.js`. For example, run `PORT=8080 HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0 node server.js` to start the server on `http://0.0.0.0:8080`.

</PagesOnly>

## Caveats

- While tracing in monorepo setups, the project directory is used for tracing by default. For `next build packages/web-app`, `packages/web-app` would be the tracing root and any files outside of that folder will not be included. To include files outside of this folder you can set `outputFileTracingRoot` in your `next.config.js`.

```js filename="packages/web-app/next.config.js"
const path = require('path')

module.exports = {
  // this includes files from the monorepo base two directories up
  outputFileTracingRoot: path.join(__dirname, '../../'),
}
```

- There are some cases in which Next.js might fail to include required files, or might incorrectly include unused files. In those cases, you can leverage `outputFileTracingExcludes` and `outputFileTracingIncludes` respectively in `next.config.js`. Each option accepts an object whose keys are **route globs** (matched with [picomatch](https://www.npmjs.com/package/picomatch#basic-globbing) against the route path, e.g. `/api/hello`) and whose values are **glob patterns resolved from the project root** that specify files to include or exclude in the trace.

> **Good to know**:
> In a monorepo, `project root` refers to the Next.js project root (the folder containing next.config.js, e.g., packages/web-app), not necessarily the monorepo root.

```js filename="next.config.js"
module.exports = {
  outputFileTracingExcludes: {
    '/api/hello': ['./un-necessary-folder/**/*'],
  },
  outputFileTracingIncludes: {
    '/api/another': ['./necessary-folder/**/*'],
    '/api/login/\\[\\[\\.\\.\\.slug\\]\\]': [
      './node_modules/aws-crt/dist/bin/**/*',
    ],
  },

````

### Assistant [commentary] (2026-05-19T16:36:59.950Z)

This is caused by `eslint-config-next` requiring a file from `next` without declaring `next` as a peer or dependency. I’m adding a pnpm package extension so the workspace installs that expected dependency edge explicitly instead of relying on root-level hoisting.

-This is a [Next.js](https://nextjs.org) project bootstrapped with [`create-next-app`](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/cli/create-next-app).
+# Agents Battle Royale
+
+This repository is organized as a pnpm workspace.
+
+- `frontend/` contains the existing Next.js application.
+- `backend/` is reserved for the future backend service.
 
 ## Getting Started
 
-First, run the development server:
+Run the frontend development server from the repository root:
 
 ```bash
-npm run dev
-# or
-yarn dev
-# or
 pnpm dev
-# or
-bun dev
 ```
 
-Open [http://localhost:3000](http://localhost:3000) with your browser to see the result.
-
-You can start editing the page by modifying `app/page.tsx`. The page auto-updates as you edit the file.
-
-This project uses [`next/font`](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/optimizing/fonts) to automatically optimize and load [Geist](https://vercel.com/font), a new font family for Vercel.
-
-## Learn More
-
-To learn more about Next.js, take a look at the following resources:
+Or run commands directly in the frontend workspace:
 
-- [Next.js Documentation](https://nextjs.org/docs) - learn about Next.js features and API.
-- [Learn Next.js](https://nextjs.org/learn) - an interactive Next.js tutorial.
-
-You can check out [the Next.js GitHub repository](https://github.com/vercel/next.js) - your feedback and contributions are welcome!
-
-## Deploy on Vercel
-
-The easiest way to deploy your Next.js app is to use the [Vercel Platform](https://vercel.com/new?utm_medium=default-template&filter=next.js&utm_source=create-next-app&utm_campaign=create-next-app-readme) from the creators of Next.js.
-
-Check out our [Next.js deployment documentation](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/deploying) for more details.
+```bash
+pnpm --filter @agents-battle-royale/frontend dev
+```
diff --git a/package.json b/package.json
index 0d07fb1..c461121 100644
--- a/package.json
+++ b/package.json
@@ -3,31 +3,9 @@
   "version": "0.1.0",
   "private": true,
   "scripts": {
-    "dev": "next dev",
-    "build": "next build",

### Assistant [commentary] (2026-05-19T16:38:05.667Z)

One more cleanup: after introducing a nested workspace, the root `.gitignore` should ignore nested `node_modules`, `.next`, `out`, and `build` directories generally, not just at the repository root. I’m tightening that now so generated frontend files do not get accidentally added.

```text
import type { Metadata } from "next";
import { Geist, Geist_Mono } from "next/font/google";
import "./globals.css";

const geistSans = Geist({
  variable: "--font-geist-sans",
  subsets: ["latin"],
});

const geistMono = Geist_Mono({
  variable: "--font-geist-mono",
  subsets: ["latin"],
});

export const metadata: Metadata = {
  title: "Create Next App",
  description: "Generated by create next app",
};

export default function RootLayout({
  children,
}: Readonly<{
  children: React.ReactNode;
}>) {
  return (
    <html
      lang="en"
      className={`${geistSans.variable} ${geistMono.variable} h-full antialiased`}
    >
      <body className="min-h-full flex flex-col">{children}</body>
    </html>
  );
}

```

````text
Chunk ID: c38aa5

---
title: Font Optimization
description: Learn how to optimize fonts in Next.js
related:
  title: API Reference
  description: See the API Reference for the full feature set of Next.js Font
  links:
    - app/api-reference/components/font
---

The [`next/font`](/docs/app/api-reference/components/font) module automatically optimizes your fonts and removes external network requests for improved privacy and performance.

It includes **built-in self-hosting** for any font file. This means you can optimally load web fonts with no layout shift.

<AppOnly>

To start using `next/font`, import it from [`next/font/local`](#local-fonts) or [`next/font/google`](#google-fonts), call it as a function with the appropriate options, and set the `className` of the element you want to apply the font to. For example:

```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" highlight={1,3-5,9} switcher
import { Geist } from 'next/font/google'

const geist = Geist({
  subsets: ['latin'],
})

export default function Layout({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  return (
    <html lang="en" className={geist.className}>
      <body>{children}</body>
    </html>
  )
}
```

```jsx filename="app/layout.js" highlight={1,3-5,9} switcher
import { Geist } from 'next/font/google'

const geist = Geist({
  subsets: ['latin'],
})

export default function Layout({ children }) {
  return (
    <html className={geist.className}>
      <body>{children}</body>
    </html>
  )
}
```

Fonts are scoped to the component they're used in. To apply a font to your entire application, add it to the [Root Layout](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout#root-layout).

</AppOnly>

<PagesOnly>

To start using `next/font`, import it from [`next/font/local`](#local-fonts) or [`next/font/google`](#google-fonts), call it as a function with the appropriate options, and set the `className` of the element you want to apply the font to. For example, you can apply fonts globally in your [Custom App](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/custom-app) (`pages/_app`):

```tsx filename="pages/_app.tsx" highlight={1,4-6,10} switcher
import { Geist } from 'next/font/google'
import type { AppProps } from 'next/app'

const geist = Geist({
  subsets: ['latin'],
})

export default function MyApp({ Component, pageProps }: AppProps) {
  return (
    <main className={geist.className}>
      <Component {...pageProps} />
    </main>
  )
}
```

```jsx filename="pages/_app.js" highlight={1,3-5,9} switcher
import { Geist } from 'next/font/google'

const geist = Geist({
  subsets: ['latin'],
})

export default function MyApp({ Component, pageProps }) {
  return (
    <main className={geist.className}>
      <Component {...pageProps} />
    </main>
  )
}
```

</PagesOnly>

## Google fonts

You can automatically self-host any Google Font. Fonts are included stored as static assets and served from the same domain as your deployment, meaning no requests are sent to Google by the browser when the user visits your site.

To start using a Google Font, import your chosen font from `next/font/google`:

<AppOnly>

```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher
import { Geist } from 'next/font/google'

const geist = Geist({
  subsets: ['latin'],
})

export default function RootLayout({
  children,
}: {
  children: React.ReactNode
}) {
  return (
    <html lang="en" className={geist.className}>
      <body>{children}</body>
    </html>
  )
}
```

```jsx filename="app/layout.js" switcher
import { Geist } from 'next/font/google'

const geist = Geist({
  subsets: ['latin'],
})

export default function RootLayout({ children }) {
  return (

````

### Assistant [final_answer] (2026-05-19T16:51:35.975Z)

Yes, `./frontend/` is effectively correct because the Next.js app lives in `frontend/`.

For most deploy UIs, I’d enter it as:

```text
frontend
```

or:

```text
./frontend
```

Both usually resolve to the same directory. If the field accepts a trailing slash, `./frontend/` is fine too, but `frontend` is the cleanest.

Reason: the repo root has the workspace files, but the actual Next app has its own [package.json](/Users/billli/Ivo/agents-battle-royale/frontend/package.json), [next.config.ts](/Users/billli/Ivo/agents-battle-royale/frontend/next.config.ts), and `app/` directory under `frontend/`.

<context>

### The Project: An AI Coding Agent Battle Royale

Goal: To build a browser-based multi-spectator game that generates AI coding agents (“competitors”) and selects the best one through a programming-challenge battle royale.

Humans don’t compete in the game. Humans just spectate and submit challenges. AI agents compete.
## Background

An agent is a piece of software that runs an LLM in a loop to achieve a goal. In this case, the goal is to answer a challenge.

  

Challenges are programming problems with known answers. To achieve their goal of answering a challenge, competitors must be able to generate and execute code.

  

Examples challenges:

- Question: What is the md5sum of “AI Battle Royale”?
    

- Answer: 4359c152baed9981d7b783b6a8bf2704
    

- Question: What is the base-16 (hex) representation of 255^10?
    

- Answer: 0xf62c88d104d1882cf601
    

- Question: What is 123456789 * 987654321?
    

- Answer: 121932631112635269
    

- Question: What is 7^77 mod 999999937?
    

- Answer: 860842589
    

- Question: What is the sum of the ASCII values of every character in the string "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog"?
    

- Answer: 4057
    

## Users

There are two types of user: spectators and admins.

- Spectator
    

- Has a username, but not necessarily a password (doesn’t have to be logged in).
    
- Can submit challenges to an active battle.
    
- Can enqueue challenges to the next battle.
    

- Admin
    

- Can do everything a spectator can do, but also has to be logged in.
    
- Can configure a battle, eg. by setting the number of competitors.
    
- Can delete/clear enqueued challenges.
    
- Can start a battle.
    

It is OK if your software only supports a single admin.

## Gameplay

The admin configures a game (e.g. by setting the number of competitors) and starts it.

  

As the game progresses, spectators submit challenges. When a challenge is submitted, a group of 2-4 competitors are randomly selected for a skirmish.

  

In a skirmish, all of the competitors race to answer the challenge as quickly as possible. Competitors are eliminated according to the following rules:

- Any competitors who submits an incorrect answer is eliminated.
    
- Any competitor who has not submitted a correct answer in 60 seconds is eliminated.
    
- If all competitors submit the correct answer within 60 seconds, the slowest competitor is eliminated.
    
- If all competitors in a skirmish are eliminated, the skirmish is canceled and all competitors are resurrected.
    

- This is to disincentivize challenges that are too hard, or have the wrong answers.
    

  

Competitors continue to be eliminated in skirmishes until only one competitor remains, who is crowned the winner.

</context>

Here is the context. My plan for building the backend for the project that pairs with a next.js frontend is the following:

Here's the current shape of the backend:

**Entry point — `index.ts`**

Bun's HTTP server handling all requests, with a router that separates admin vs. public endpoints, and WebSocket upgrade handling.

**`GameManager` singleton**

The heart of the backend. Holds all live state in memory:

ts

```ts
class GameManager {
  battle: Battle | null
  competitors: Competitor[]
  challengeQueue: Challenge[]
  activeSkirmishes: Map<string, Skirmish>
  spectators: Set<ServerWebSocket>
}
```

Responsible for starting battles, spawning skirmishes, applying elimination rules, and broadcasting events to all connected WebSocket clients.

**Agent runner — `agent.ts`**

A function that takes a challenge and a competitor, runs the Vercel AI SDK `generateText` loop with a `executeCode` tool, and resolves with the answer and time taken. Each skirmish calls this concurrently for 2-4 competitors via `Promise.allSettled` with a 60-second `AbortSignal`.

**Code executor — `executor.ts`**

The function wired up as the AI tool. Takes generated code, runs it safely (child process with a timeout), and returns stdout. Called by the agent runner.

**Routes**

```
POST /auth/login              # returns JWT
GET  /game/state              # current battle state snapshot
POST /challenges/submit       # spectator submits a challenge to active battle
POST /challenges/enqueue      # spectator enqueues for next battle
DELETE /challenges/:id        # admin only
POST /battle/start            # admin only
POST /battle/configure        # admin only
GET  /ws                      # WebSocket upgrade
```

**WebSocket events (server → client)**

ts

```ts
{ type: "skirmish:started", competitors, challenge }
{ type: "skirmish:result", eliminated, survivors }
{ type: "battle:winner", competitor }
{ type: "competitor:eliminated", competitor }
{ type: "challenge:queued", challenge }
```

**File structure**

```
backend/
├── index.ts          # Bun server + router
├── game/
│   ├── GameManager.ts
│   ├── agent.ts
│   └── executor.ts
├── routes/
│   ├── auth.ts
│   ├── battle.ts
│   └── challenges.ts
└── types.ts          # shared with frontend via /shared
```

The key insight is that `GameManager` is the only stateful piece — everything else is just functions that read from or write to it. Keeps it easy to reason about for a 24-hour build.

---

What are your thoughts on this?  are there any potential rabbit holes that I am missing here?  what would you change to make this better?

### Assistant [final_answer] (2026-05-19T17:01:03.823Z)

I checked the repo: `backend/` is not scaffolded yet, so your plan is effectively greenfield. The architecture is good for a 24-hour build, especially the “single `GameManager` owns live state” idea. I’d keep that. The main risks are not routing or file structure; they’re isolation, race conditions, and game-state semantics.

Biggest rabbit holes I’d watch:

1. **Code execution security**
   A child process with timeout is not enough if agents can run arbitrary code. They can fork, consume memory, read env vars, access filesystem, hang on I/O, or hit the network. For a hackathon MVP, either:
   - only allow a tiny interpreted sandbox you control, or
   - run code in a locked-down Docker/container sandbox with CPU/memory/time limits, no network, no secrets mounted.

2. **Concurrent skirmish races**
   If multiple spectators submit challenges at once, the same competitor may get selected into multiple skirmishes unless you model competitor state. I’d add:
   ```ts
   status: "active" | "in_skirmish" | "eliminated"
   ```
   Then only select `"active"` idle competitors. Otherwise elimination results can conflict.

3. **Battle state machine**
   Make battle lifecycle explicit:
   ```ts
   "configuring" | "active" | "finished"
   ```
   This avoids weird cases like submitting active challenges before a battle exists, starting twice, configuring mid-battle, or declaring a winner while a skirmish is still resolving.

4. **Challenge answer handling**
   Spectator-submitted challenges need known answers, but agents must never see them. I’d store:
   ```ts
   { id, prompt, expectedAnswer, submittedBy, status }
   ```
   Broadcast only `prompt`, never `expectedAnswer`.

5. **All-eliminated rule**
   Define this precisely before coding. I’d interpret it as: if every competitor in that skirmish would be eliminated, cancel the skirmish and restore only those skirmish participants to their previous active state. Do not resurrect the whole battle unless that is intentionally part of the game design.

6. **LLM cost and fan-out**
   One challenge can trigger 2-4 agent loops, each with tool calls and code execution. A few spectators can accidentally create a lot of spend. Add basic throttles:
   - one active skirmish at a time for MVP, or a small max concurrency
   - per-username/IP submit cooldown
   - queue active challenges instead of launching unlimited skirmishes

7. **WebSocket reliability**
   WebSocket events are good, but clients will miss events on refresh/reconnect. Keep `GET /game/state` as the source of truth, and include monotonic event IDs:
   ```ts
   { seq, type, payload }
   ```
   The frontend can refetch state after reconnect.

8. **Agent cancellation**
   The `AbortSignal` should propagate through `generateText`, tool execution, and child process cleanup. Otherwise timed-out agents may continue running code after the skirmish is over.

What I’d change:

- Add `shared/` now for typed DTOs/events, even if small.
- Add `Battle.status`, `Competitor.status`, and a simple skirmish scheduler.
- Treat active challenge submissions as queued work internally, even if they feel instant to users.
- Make `executor.ts` the most conservative part of the system.
- Keep auth dead simple: one admin password from env, JWT or signed cookie, no user DB.
- Add a minimal event log or `seq` counter in `GameManager`.

I’d keep your broad structure, but I’d slightly reshape it around a state machine:

```ts
backend/
├── index.ts
├── game/
│   ├── GameManager.ts
│   ├── scheduler.ts
│   ├── agent.ts
│   └── executor.ts
├── routes/
│   ├── auth.ts
│   ├── battle.ts
│   └── challenges.ts
└── shared/
    └── types.ts
```

The main thing: avoid making “submit challenge” directly run all agent promises inline. Have it enqueue a skirmish request, then let `GameManager`/scheduler decide when it is valid to start. That one choice will prevent most of the ugly race conditions.

### Assistant [final_answer] (2026-05-19T17:01:03.827Z)

I checked the repo: `backend/` is not scaffolded yet, so your plan is effectively greenfield. The architecture is good for a 24-hour build, especially the “single `GameManager` owns live state” idea. I’d keep that. The main risks are not routing or file structure; they’re isolation, race conditions, and game-state semantics.

Biggest rabbit holes I’d watch:

1. **Code execution security**
   A child process with timeout is not enough if agents can run arbitrary code. They can fork, consume memory, read env vars, access filesystem, hang on I/O, or hit the network. For a hackathon MVP, either:
   - only allow a tiny interpreted sandbox you control, or
   - run code in a locked-down Docker/container sandbox with CPU/memory/time limits, no network, no secrets mounted.

2. **Concurrent skirmish races**
   If multiple spectators submit challenges at once, the same competitor may get selected into multiple skirmishes unless you model competitor state. I’d add:
   ```ts
   status: "active" | "in_skirmish" | "eliminated"
   ```
   Then only select `"active"` idle competitors. Otherwise elimination results can conflict.

3. **Battle state machine**
   Make battle lifecycle explicit:
   ```ts
   "configuring" | "active" | "finished"
   ```
   This avoids weird cases like submitting active challenges before a battle exists, starting twice, configuring mid-battle, or declaring a winner while a skirmish is still resolving.

4. **Challenge answer handling**
   Spectator-submitted challenges need known answers, but agents must never see them. I’d store:
   ```ts
   { id, prompt, expectedAnswer, submittedBy, status }
   ```
   Broadcast only `prompt`, never `expectedAnswer`.

5. **All-eliminated rule**
   Define this precisely before coding. I’d interpret it as: if every competitor in that skirmish would be eliminated, cancel the skirmish and restore only those skirmish participants to their previous active state. Do not resurrect the whole battle unless that is intentionally part of the game design.

6. **LLM cost and fan-out**
   One challenge can trigger 2-4 agent loops, each with tool calls and code execution. A few spectators can accidentally create a lot of spend. Add basic throttles:
   - one active skirmish at a time for MVP, or a small max concurrency
   - per-username/IP submit cooldown
   - queue active challenges instead of launching unlimited skirmishes

7. **WebSocket reliability**
   WebSocket events are good, but clients will miss events on refresh/reconnect. Keep `GET /game/state` as the source of truth, and include monotonic event IDs:
   ```ts
   { seq, type, payload }
   ```
   The frontend can refetch state after reconnect.

8. **Agent cancellation**
   The `AbortSignal` should propagate through `generateText`, tool execution, and child process cleanup. Otherwise timed-out agents may continue running code after the skirmish is over.

What I’d change:

- Add `shared/` now for typed DTOs/events, even if small.
- Add `Battle.status`, `Competitor.status`, and a simple skirmish scheduler.
- Treat active challenge submissions as queued work internally, even if they feel instant to users.
- Make `executor.ts` the most conservative part of the system.
- Keep auth dead simple: one admin password from env, JWT or signed cookie, no user DB.
- Add a minimal event log or `seq` counter in `GameManager`.

I’d keep your broad structure, but I’d slightly reshape it around a state machine:

```ts
backend/
├── index.ts
├── game/
│   ├── GameManager.ts
│   ├── scheduler.ts
│   ├── agent.ts
│   └── executor.ts
├── routes/
│   ├── auth.ts
│   ├── battle.ts
│   └── challenges.ts
└── shared/
    └── types.ts
```

The main thing: avoid making “submit challenge” directly run all agent promises inline. Have it enqueue a skirmish request, then let `GameManager`/scheduler decide when it is valid to start. That one choice will prevent most of the ugly race conditions.

### User (2026-05-19T17:08:50.903Z)

I asked my coworker the same thing, and these are his notes:

Claude responded: Good plan overall — the single-stateful-singleton approach is exactly right for a 24-hour build.Good plan overall — the single-stateful-singleton approach is exactly right for a 24-hour build. Here are my honest thoughts:
What's solid
The GameManager as the single source of truth is the right call. Promise.allSettled with AbortSignal for concurrent agent racing is clean. The WebSocket event shape is minimal and sensible.

Rabbit holes to watch
1. The code executor is your biggest risk. Sandboxing arbitrary LLM-generated code in a child process is harder than it looks. child_process with a timeout doesn't protect against: network calls leaking out, filesystem writes, infinite memory allocation before the timeout fires, or fork bombs. For a 24-hour build I'd strongly recommend just using --allow-none or a Deno subprocess instead of Node, since Deno's permission flags give you a much tighter sandbox with zero extra work. Alternatively, just bun run --smol with a memory cap. Don't let this become a rabbit hole — pick one approach and accept its limitations.
2. Skirmish concurrency state is subtle. When a skirmish resolves, you need to handle the case where a competitor was already eliminated by a different concurrent skirmish that finished milliseconds earlier. Your elimination logic needs a "is this competitor still alive?" check at resolution time, not just at start time. Easy to miss in the happy path.
3. The "all eliminated → resurrect" rule is stateful in a tricky way. You need to track which competitors were in that skirmish and uneliminate them atomically. Make sure GameManager has a clear eliminateCompetitor / resurrectCompetitor method rather than mutating the array inline — otherwise this gets messy fast.
4. Agent identity/personality. The spec says the system "generates" competitors — are these just names/numbers, or do they have distinct system prompts / strategies? If you want the spectacle of watching different agents behave differently, you'll want at least a small personality bank baked in from the start. Easy to add later, but harder to retrofit into the WebSocket event schema.

Things I'd change
Add skirmish:update events. Right now you only broadcast start and result. Spectators watching a 60-second skirmish will see nothing for up to a minute. Even a { type: "agent:thinking", competitor, tokens_so_far } or { type: "agent:executing_code", competitor } intermediate event makes the UI dramatically more engaging and is basically free to emit from agent.ts.
Separate Challenge submission validation. Right now POST /challenges/submit presumably triggers a skirmish inline. I'd decouple these: submit goes into a queue, and GameManager has a tick() or event-driven loop that picks up queued challenges and spawns skirmishes. This prevents a slow challenge submission from blocking the HTTP response and makes it easier to throttle skirmish concurrency (you probably don't want 10 simultaneous skirmishes).
Track skirmish history on the Battle object. You'll want this for the frontend leaderboard/replay and it costs nothing to append to an array as skirmishes resolve.
One thing to drop: the DELETE /challenges/:id endpoint is probably not worth implementing in 24 hours. A DELETE /challenges/all (clear queue) is what an admin actually needs mid-game, and it's one line.

Overall verdict: This is a well-scoped plan. The executor sandbox and concurrent elimination state are the two places most likely to eat time — I'd timebox both explicitly before you start.

---

Obviously there's some overlap here but what do you think is valid, what is not? What would you incorporate into your plan?

### Assistant [final_answer] (2026-05-19T17:09:07.139Z)

Your coworker’s notes are mostly valid. I’d incorporate several of them directly, with a couple of caveats.

**Definitely Valid**
The executor warning is correct. This is the biggest technical risk. I agree with the direction, but I’d be careful with the exact prescription:

- **Deno with permission flags** is a good MVP sandbox choice if you can install/run it easily:
  ```bash
  deno run --no-net --no-read --no-write --no-env --no-ffi --no-run file.ts
  ```
  Add subprocess timeout and ideally memory/process limits too.
- `bun run --smol` is not enough as a security boundary. It may reduce memory behavior, but it does not sandbox filesystem/network/env access in the same way.
- A Node child process with timeout is fine only for trusted code. For LLM-generated code, I would not rely on it.

Skirmish concurrency is also a real risk. I’d avoid the “competitor eliminated by another skirmish” problem by not allowing a competitor to be in two skirmishes at once. Add `competitor.status = "active" | "in_skirmish" | "eliminated"` and only select `active` competitors. That is simpler than trying to reconcile overlapping results.

The “all eliminated -> resurrect” rule does need centralized methods. I’d add `eliminateCompetitor`, `restoreCompetitors`, and `resolveSkirmish` methods on `GameManager`. No scattered mutation.

Intermediate events are a good suggestion. Spectators need motion. I’d add these:

```ts
{ type: "skirmish:started", skirmish }
{ type: "competitor:started", skirmishId, competitorId }
{ type: "competitor:tool_call", skirmishId, competitorId }
{ type: "competitor:answered", skirmishId, competitorId, correct, elapsedMs }
{ type: "skirmish:resolved", skirmish }
```

I would not stream raw tokens at first. It complicates UI, privacy, and event volume. Tool-call/status events give most of the spectator value with much less mess.

Decoupling challenge submission from skirmish execution is also worth doing. `POST /challenges/submit` should accept and return quickly, then the scheduler starts a skirmish when capacity exists.

Skirmish history is worth adding from the start. Not a complex replay system, just append resolved skirmish summaries to `battle.skirmishes`.

**Partly Valid**
Agent identity/personality is useful for spectacle, but I’d keep it lightweight. Add fields now:

```ts
type Competitor = {
  id: string
  name: string
  avatar?: string
  strategy: string
  status: CompetitorStatus
}
```

Then use `strategy` in the system prompt. Don’t build a full agent-personality system yet.

The “DELETE `/challenges/:id` isn’t worth it” point depends on admin UX. I agree that `DELETE /challenges/all` is more useful for a 24-hour MVP. But deleting one bad queued challenge is cheap if your route structure already supports it. I’d prioritize:

```txt
DELETE /challenges/queue
```

Then add per-id delete only if time remains.

**I Would Incorporate**
I’d update the backend plan like this:

- Keep `GameManager` as the only stateful owner.
- Add explicit battle and competitor statuses.
- Add a small scheduler inside `GameManager` instead of launching skirmishes directly inside HTTP handlers.
- Use Deno or a similarly restricted subprocess for code execution, with timeout and output limits.
- Add skirmish history.
- Add intermediate WebSocket events, but status-level only, not token streaming.
- Add personality/strategy as a simple competitor field.
- Prefer clear queue over individual challenge deletion for MVP.

The most important design change is this: **make skirmishes non-overlapping per competitor, and ideally globally limited at first**. For a 24-hour build, one active skirmish at a time is completely defensible. It makes the game easier to understand, easier to debug, and easier to broadcast. You can raise concurrency later once the core loop works.

### Assistant [final_answer] (2026-05-19T17:09:07.141Z)

Your coworker’s notes are mostly valid. I’d incorporate several of them directly, with a couple of caveats.

**Definitely Valid**
The executor warning is correct. This is the biggest technical risk. I agree with the direction, but I’d be careful with the exact prescription:

- **Deno with permission flags** is a good MVP sandbox choice if you can install/run it easily:
  ```bash
  deno run --no-net --no-read --no-write --no-env --no-ffi --no-run file.ts
  ```
  Add subprocess timeout and ideally memory/process limits too.
- `bun run --smol` is not enough as a security boundary. It may reduce memory behavior, but it does not sandbox filesystem/network/env access in the same way.
- A Node child process with timeout is fine only for trusted code. For LLM-generated code, I would not rely on it.

Skirmish concurrency is also a real risk. I’d avoid the “competitor eliminated by another skirmish” problem by not allowing a competitor to be in two skirmishes at once. Add `competitor.status = "active" | "in_skirmish" | "eliminated"` and only select `active` competitors. That is simpler than trying to reconcile overlapping results.

The “all eliminated -> resurrect” rule does need centralized methods. I’d add `eliminateCompetitor`, `restoreCompetitors`, and `resolveSkirmish` methods on `GameManager`. No scattered mutation.

Intermediate events are a good suggestion. Spectators need motion. I’d add these:

```ts
{ type: "skirmish:started", skirmish }
{ type: "competitor:started", skirmishId, competitorId }
{ type: "competitor:tool_call", skirmishId, competitorId }
{ type: "competitor:answered", skirmishId, competitorId, correct, elapsedMs }
{ type: "skirmish:resolved", skirmish }
```

I would not stream raw tokens at first. It complicates UI, privacy, and event volume. Tool-call/status events give most of the spectator value with much less mess.

Decoupling challenge submission from skirmish execution is also worth doing. `POST /challenges/submit` should accept and return quickly, then the scheduler starts a skirmish when capacity exists.

Skirmish history is worth adding from the start. Not a complex replay system, just append resolved skirmish summaries to `battle.skirmishes`.

**Partly Valid**
Agent identity/personality is useful for spectacle, but I’d keep it lightweight. Add fields now:

```ts
type Competitor = {
  id: string
  name: string
  avatar?: string
  strategy: string
  status: CompetitorStatus
}
```

Then use `strategy` in the system prompt. Don’t build a full agent-personality system yet.

The “DELETE `/challenges/:id` isn’t worth it” point depends on admin UX. I agree that `DELETE /challenges/all` is more useful for a 24-hour MVP. But deleting one bad queued challenge is cheap if your route structure already supports it. I’d prioritize:

```txt
DELETE /challenges/queue
```

Then add per-id delete only if time remains.

**I Would Incorporate**
I’d update the backend plan like this:

- Keep `GameManager` as the only stateful owner.
- Add explicit battle and competitor statuses.
- Add a small scheduler inside `GameManager` instead of launching skirmishes directly inside HTTP handlers.
- Use Deno or a similarly restricted subprocess for code execution, with timeout and output limits.
- Add skirmish history.
- Add intermediate WebSocket events, but status-level only, not token streaming.
- Add personality/strategy as a simple competitor field.
- Prefer clear queue over individual challenge deletion for MVP.

The most important design change is this: **make skirmishes non-overlapping per competitor, and ideally globally limited at first**. For a 24-hour build, one active skirmish at a time is completely defensible. It makes the game easier to understand, easier to debug, and easier to broadcast. You can raise concurrency later once the core loop works.

### User (2026-05-19T17:10:43.389Z)

Can we not just make skirmishes one at a time? For example if there are ten agents in a battle and a skirmish selects four of them, the other six can just "spectate". However at any given time there will only be one active skirmish. Does this make sense?

### Assistant [final_answer] (2026-05-19T17:10:50.253Z)

Yes. For this project, I think **one active skirmish at a time is the better default**.

It makes the backend much simpler:

- No competitor can be in two skirmishes at once.
- Elimination order is deterministic.
- The “all eliminated -> resurrect” rule is easier to apply.
- WebSocket state is easier for the frontend to render.
- LLM/tool execution costs are naturally capped.
- Challenge submissions can return immediately and wait in a queue.

I’d model it like this:

```ts
class GameManager {
  battle: Battle | null
  activeSkirmish: Skirmish | null
  pendingChallenges: Challenge[]
}
```

Then the flow is:

1. Spectator submits a challenge.
2. Backend validates it and pushes it into `pendingChallenges`.
3. `GameManager.maybeStartNextSkirmish()` runs.
4. If `activeSkirmish !== null`, do nothing.
5. If no active skirmish exists, pop the next challenge, randomly select 2-4 active competitors, and start the skirmish.
6. When the skirmish resolves, apply eliminations, append skirmish history, clear `activeSkirmish`, check for winner, then call `maybeStartNextSkirmish()` again.

You can still show the other six agents as spectators/idle competitors in the UI. They are simply not involved in that skirmish.

I’d still keep a competitor status, but it becomes simpler:

```ts
type CompetitorStatus = "active" | "competing" | "eliminated"
```

At most 2-4 competitors are `"competing"` at a time. Everyone else is `"active"` or `"eliminated"`.

This also makes the queue semantics clearer:

```txt
POST /challenges/submit
```

does not mean “start a skirmish right now.” It means “submit this challenge to be used by the skirmish scheduler.”

So yes: this makes sense, and I’d incorporate it into the plan. It is the right tradeoff for a first working version.

### Assistant [final_answer] (2026-05-19T17:12:40.160Z)

### Assistant [final_answer] (2026-05-19T17:12:40.160Z)

I’m still in Plan Mode, so I can’t write the markdown file to disk yet. Here is the complete file content I would put at `backend/BACKEND_PLAN.md` once implementation/editing is enabled.

<proposed_plan>
# Backend Implementation Plan

## Summary

Build a Bun-based backend for the AI Coding Agent Battle Royale with one in-memory `GameManager` as the source of truth. The backend will expose HTTP routes for auth, battle control, challenge submission, and state snapshots, plus a WebSocket endpoint for live spectator updates.

The first version will run exactly one skirmish at a time. Submitted challenges enter a queue, and the scheduler starts the next skirmish only when no skirmish is active. This keeps elimination order deterministic, caps LLM/tool execution cost, and avoids concurrent state conflicts.

## Architecture

### Runtime And Package Shape

- Add a `backend/` workspace package using Bun and TypeScript.
- Keep backend state in memory for the MVP.
- Share public DTO/event types with the frontend through a shared TypeScript module.
- Use environment variables for secrets and provider credentials.

Proposed structure:

```txt
backend/
├── BACKEND_PLAN.md
├── package.json
├── tsconfig.json
├── index.ts
├── game/
│   ├── GameManager.ts
│   ├── agent.ts
│   ├── executor.ts
│   ├── competitors.ts
│   └── ids.ts
├── routes/
│   ├── auth.ts
│   ├── battle.ts
│   ├── challenges.ts
│   └── state.ts
├── shared/
│   └── types.ts
└── test/
    ├── GameManager.test.ts
    ├── battleLifecycle.test.ts
    └── executor.test.ts
```

### Core Design

`GameManager` owns all mutable live game state:

```ts
class GameManager {
  battle: Battle | null;
  activeSkirmish: Skirmish | null;
  pendingChallenges: Challenge[];
  spectators: Set<ServerWebSocket>;
}
```

HTTP routes validate requests and call `GameManager` methods. They should not directly mutate battle, competitor, challenge, or skirmish state.

Agent execution and code execution stay behind narrow functional interfaces:

```ts
runAgentForChallenge(input): Promise<AgentResult>
executeCode(input, signal): Promise<ExecutionResult>
```

## Public Types

Define public frontend-safe types in `backend/shared/types.ts`.

### Battle

```ts
type BattleStatus = "configuring" | "active" | "finished";

type Battle = {
  id: string;
  status: BattleStatus;
  configuredCompetitorCount: number;
  competitors: Competitor[];
  skirmishHistory: SkirmishSummary[];
  winner: CompetitorPublic | null;
  createdAt: string;
  startedAt: string | null;
  finishedAt: string | null;
};
```

### Competitor

```ts
type CompetitorStatus = "active" | "competing" | "eliminated";

type Competitor = {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  strategy: string;
  status: CompetitorStatus;
  eliminatedAt: string | null;
};
```

`strategy` is used in the competitor system prompt to create visible variation between agents. Keep it simple for v1: a short prompt fragment selected from a fixed bank.

### Challenge

```ts
type Challenge = {
  id: string;
  prompt: string;
  expectedAnswer: string;
  submittedBy: string;
  status: "queued" | "running" | "completed" | "canceled";
  createdAt: string;
};
```

Important: `expectedAnswer` is never sent to spectators or agents.

Frontend-facing challenge DTO:

```ts
type ChallengePublic = Omit<Challenge, "expectedAnswer">;
```

### Skirmish

```ts
type Skirmish = {
  id: string;
  battleId: string;
  challenge: Challenge;
  competitorIds: string[];
  status: "running" | "resolved" | "canceled";
  startedAt: string;
  resolvedAt: string | null;
  results: SkirmishCompetitorResult[];
};
```

```ts
type SkirmishCompetitorResult = {
  competitorId: string;
  answer: string | null;
  correct: boolean;
  elapsedMs: number | null;
  error: string | null;
};
```

### State Snapshot

`GET /game/state` returns the full frontend-safe state:

```ts
type GameState = {
  battle: BattlePublic | null;
  activeSkirmish: SkirmishPublic | null;
  pendingChallenges: ChallengePublic[];
  seq: number;
};
```

`seq` is a monotonically increasing event counter used by clients to detect missed live updates.

## HTTP API

### Public Routes

```txt
GET  /game/state
POST /challenges/submit
POST /challenges/enqueue
GET  /ws
```

For MVP, `/challenges/submit` and `/challenges/enqueue` can share the same queue behavior:

- If a battle is active, the challenge enters the active pending queue.
- If no battle is active, the challenge remains queued for the next battle.
- The frontend can present these differently, but the backend can store them in the same queue initially.

`POST /challenges/submit` body:

```ts
{
  username: string;
  prompt: string;
  expectedAnswer: string;
}
```

Validation:

- `username`: required, trimmed, max 40 characters.
- `prompt`: required, trimmed, max 2,000 characters.
- `expectedAnswer`: required, trimmed, max 500 characters.
- Reject empty strings after trim.

Response:

```ts
{
  challenge: ChallengePublic;
  state: GameState;
}
```

### Admin Routes

```txt
POST   /auth/login
POST   /battle/configure
POST   /battle/start
DELETE /challenges/queue
DELETE /challenges/:id
```

`POST /auth/login`:

- Accept one admin password from `ADMIN_PASSWORD`.
- Return a signed JWT or signed opaque token.
- Use `Authorization: Bearer <token>` for admin routes.
- A single admin account is sufficient.

`POST /battle/configure` body:

```ts
{
  competitorCount: number;
}
```

Validation:

- Only allowed when no active battle exists or current battle is `"configuring"` / `"finished"`.
- Recommended competitor count range: 4 to 32.
- Store the configuration but do not start the battle.

`POST /battle/start`:

- Creates a new battle if none exists or if the previous battle is finished.
- Generates competitors from the configured count.
- Sets battle status to `"active"`.
- Calls `maybeStartNextSkirmish()` after starting.

`DELETE /challenges/queue`:

- Admin clears all pending challenges.
- Active skirmish is not affected.

`DELETE /challenges/:id`:

- Admin deletes one queued challenge if it is still pending.
- If the challenge is already running or completed, return a 409 conflict.

## WebSocket Events

All server-to-client events include `seq` and `state`-safe payloads.

```ts
type ServerEvent =
  | { seq: number; type: "state:snapshot"; state: GameState }
  | { seq: number; type: "challenge:queued"; challenge: ChallengePublic }
  | { seq: number; type: "battle:started"; battle: BattlePublic }
  | { seq: number; type: "skirmish:started"; skirmish: SkirmishPublic }
  | { seq: number; type: "competitor:started"; skirmishId: string; competitorId: string }
  | { seq: number; type: "competitor:tool_call"; skirmishId: string; competitorId: string }
  | { seq: number; type: "competitor:answered"; skirmishId: string; competitorId: string; correct: boolean; elapsedMs: number | null }
  | { seq: number; type: "competitor:eliminated"; competitor: CompetitorPublic }
  | { seq: number; type: "skirmish:resolved"; skirmish: SkirmishSummary }
  | { seq: number; type: "skirmish:canceled"; skirmish: SkirmishSummary }
  | { seq: number; type: "battle:winner"; competitor: CompetitorPublic };
```

Do not stream raw model tokens in v1. Status-level events are enough for spectator engagement and simpler to render.

On WebSocket connect:

- Add socket to `spectators`.
- Immediately send `state:snapshot`.
- Remove socket on close.

If a send fails, remove that socket from the set.

## GameManager Behavior

### Battle Start

`startBattle()`:

1. Verify no battle is currently active.
2. Generate competitors with names and strategy prompts.
3. Create a `Battle` with status `"active"`.
4. Broadcast `battle:started`.
5. Call `maybeStartNextSkirmish()`.

### Challenge Submission

`submitChallenge(input)`:

1. Validate and normalize input.
2. Create a `Challenge`.
3. Push it to `pendingChallenges`.
4. Broadcast `challenge:queued`.
5. Call `maybeStartNextSkirmish()`.
6. Return public challenge and state snapshot.

### Skirmish Scheduler

`maybeStartNextSkirmish()`:

1. Return if no active battle.
2. Return if `activeSkirmish` is not null.
3. Return if battle status is not `"active"`.
4. Return if fewer than 2 active competitors remain.
5. Return if `pendingChallenges` is empty.
6. Pop the oldest pending challenge.
7. Select 2-4 active competitors at random.
8. Mark selected competitors as `"competing"`.
9. Create `activeSkirmish`.
10. Broadcast `skirmish:started`.
11. Start async skirmish execution.
12. Do not block the HTTP request that triggered scheduling.

Selection rule:

- If 2 active competitors remain, select both.
- If 3 active competitors remain, select 2 or 3 randomly.
- If 4+ active competitors remain, select a random size from 2 to 4, then select that many competitors.

### Skirmish Resolution

Each selected competitor runs concurrently with a 60-second limit.

Resolution rules:

1. Incorrect answer means that competitor is marked for elimination.
2. Timeout means that competitor is marked for elimination.
3. If all competitors answer correctly within 60 seconds, eliminate the slowest correct competitor.
4. If every selected competitor would be eliminated, cancel the skirmish and restore all selected competitors to `"active"`.
5. Otherwise, eliminate marked competitors and restore remaining selected competitors to `"active"`.
6. Append a `SkirmishSummary` to `battle.skirmishHistory`.
7. Clear `activeSkirmish`.
8. Check for winner.
9. If no winner, call `maybeStartNextSkirmish()`.

Winner rule:

- If exactly one competitor has status `"active"` or `"competing"` after a resolved skirmish, mark battle `"finished"` and broadcast `battle:winner`.

### All-Eliminated Cancellation

For the all-eliminated case:

- Mark challenge status `"canceled"`.
- Mark skirmish status `"canceled"`.
- Restore selected competitors to `"active"`.
- Append canceled skirmish summary to history.
- Broadcast `skirmish:canceled`.
- Do not eliminate anyone.
- Continue to the next queued challenge.

This interprets “all competitors in a skirmish are eliminated” as only the participants in that skirmish, not every competitor in the battle.

## Agent Runner

`agent.ts` owns LLM interaction.

Input:

```ts
type RunAgentInput = {
  competitor: Competitor;
  challengePrompt: string;
  signal: AbortSignal;
  onEvent: (event: AgentRuntimeEvent) => void;
};
```

Output:

```ts
type AgentResult = {
  competitorId: string;
  answer: string | null;
  elapsedMs: number;
  error: string | null;
};
```

Behavior:

- Use Vercel AI SDK `generateText`.
- Provide a system prompt that includes the competitor strategy.
- Provide the challenge prompt.
- Register one tool: `executeCode`.
- The agent must return a final answer string.
- Trim the answer before comparison.
- Propagate `AbortSignal` to model call and code execution.
- Emit runtime events for:
  - started
  - tool call
  - answer submitted
  - timeout/error

Do not include `expectedAnswer` anywhere in the agent prompt, tool input, logs sent to clients, or WebSocket payloads.

## Code Executor

`executor.ts` is the highest-risk component and should stay narrow.

Preferred MVP sandbox:

- Run generated code through Deno with restrictive permissions.
- Use no network, no filesystem, no environment, no subprocess, no FFI.
- Add a wall-clock timeout.
- Limit stdout/stderr size.
- Return only text output and structured error metadata.

Example execution policy:

```txt
deno run
  --no-net
  --no-read
  --no-write
  --no-env
  --no-ffi
  --no-run
```

Executor input:

```ts
type ExecuteCodeInput = {
  code: string;
  language: "typescript" | "javascript";
  signal: AbortSignal;
};
```

Executor output:

```ts
type ExecutionResult = {
  stdout: string;
  stderr: string;
  exitCode: number | null;
  timedOut: boolean;
};
```

Limits:

- Code length max: 10,000 characters.
- Timeout: 5 seconds per tool execution.
- Output max: 20,000 characters combined stdout/stderr.
- No secrets or provider environment variables available to the subprocess.
- Temporary files should be created outside repo-tracked paths and removed after execution.

If Deno is unavailable, fallback is a Bun child process with timeout and output limits, but this is not a real sandbox. Treat that fallback as local-demo-only.

## Error Handling

Use consistent JSON error responses:

```ts
{
  error: {
    code: string;
    message: string;
  }
}
```

Recommended codes:

```txt
BAD_REQUEST
UNAUTHORIZED
FORBIDDEN
NOT_FOUND
CONFLICT
BATTLE_NOT_ACTIVE
NO_ACTIVE_COMPETITORS
INTERNAL_ERROR
```

HTTP status mapping:

- `400`: validation errors
- `401`: missing/invalid admin token
- `403`: valid user but not allowed
- `404`: missing resource
- `409`: invalid state transition
- `500`: unexpected failure

Do not expose raw LLM/provider errors or executor internals to spectators.

## Authentication

Admin authentication:

- `ADMIN_PASSWORD` required in production-like usage.
- `JWT_SECRET` required for token signing.
- `POST /auth/login` returns a token when password matches.
- Admin middleware verifies bearer token.
- No spectator login needed.

Spectator identity:

- Accept a `username` field on challenge submission.
- No password.
- No persistence.
- The frontend may store username locally.

## Configuration

Required environment variables:

```txt
ADMIN_PASSWORD=
JWT_SECRET=
OPENAI_API_KEY= or provider-specific key
```

Optional environment variables:

```txt
PORT=4000
DEFAULT_COMPETITOR_COUNT=10
MAX_COMPETITOR_COUNT=32
SKIRMISH_TIMEOUT_MS=60000
EXECUTOR_TIMEOUT_MS=5000
MAX_PENDING_CHALLENGES=100
```

Defaults should be safe for local development.

## Testing Plan

### Unit Tests

`GameManager`:

- Starts a battle with configured competitor count.
- Rejects starting a battle while one is active.
- Queues challenges.
- Starts only one skirmish at a time.
- Selects 2-4 competitors.
- Restores non-eliminated competitors after a skirmish.
- Eliminates incorrect competitors.
- Eliminates timed-out competitors.
- Eliminates slowest competitor when all are correct.
- Cancels skirmish and resurrects participants when all would be eliminated.
- Declares winner when one competitor remains.
- Does not expose `expectedAnswer` in public state.

Routes:

- Public state endpoint returns safe DTOs.
- Admin routes reject missing/invalid tokens.
- Challenge validation rejects empty or oversized fields.
- Queue clear removes pending challenges only.

Executor:

- Executes simple arithmetic code.
- Times out infinite loops.
- Truncates excessive output.
- Rejects oversized code.
- Does not expose environment variables under the Deno sandbox.

### Integration Tests

- Submit a challenge while no skirmish is active and verify one starts.
- Submit multiple challenges and verify they run sequentially.
- Connect WebSocket and verify initial `state:snapshot`.
- Verify event ordering by increasing `seq`.
- Verify refresh/reconnect can recover state via `GET /game/state`.

### Manual Acceptance Scenarios

- Admin configures 10 competitors and starts a battle.
- Spectator submits a challenge.
- UI sees `challenge:queued`, `skirmish:started`, competitor progress events, and `skirmish:resolved`.
- Repeated challenges eventually produce a winner.
- Admin can clear queued challenges without interrupting active skirmish.
- Bad challenge where all participants fail cancels the skirmish and restores participants.

## Implementation Milestones

1. Backend package setup with Bun, TypeScript, and workspace scripts.
2. Shared types and DTO helpers.
3. `GameManager` state machine without real LLM calls.
4. HTTP router and admin auth.
5. WebSocket connection management and event broadcast.
6. Deterministic fake agent runner for testing game flow.
7. Real Vercel AI SDK agent runner.
8. Deno-based executor.
9. Tests for lifecycle, queueing, elimination, and public state safety.
10. Frontend integration against `GET /game/state` and WebSocket events.

## Assumptions

- The MVP is single-process and stores all game state in memory.
- Only one active skirmish runs at a time.
- Other active competitors effectively spectate while a skirmish runs.
- The all-eliminated resurrection rule restores only that skirmish’s participants.
- Spectators do not authenticate.
- A single admin password is sufficient.
- Deno is the preferred executor sandbox for generated code.
- Raw LLM token streaming is out of scope for v1.
- Challenge answers are exact string matches after trimming whitespace.
- Persistence, multi-server scaling, durable replay, and production-grade sandboxing are out of scope for the first backend version.
</proposed_plan>

````text
# Backend Implementation Plan

## Summary

Build a Bun-based backend for the AI Coding Agent Battle Royale with one in-memory `GameManager` as the source of truth. The backend will expose HTTP routes for auth, battle control, challenge submission, and state snapshots, plus a WebSocket endpoint for live spectator updates.

The first version will run exactly one skirmish at a time. Submitted challenges enter a queue, and the scheduler starts the next skirmish only when no skirmish is active. This keeps elimination order deterministic, caps LLM/tool execution cost, and avoids concurrent state conflicts.

## Architecture

### Runtime And Package Shape

- Add a `backend/` workspace package using Bun and TypeScript.
- Keep backend state in memory for the MVP.
- Share public DTO/event types with the frontend through a shared TypeScript module.
- Use environment variables for secrets and provider credentials.

Proposed structure:

```txt
backend/
├── backend_plan.md
├── package.json
├── tsconfig.json
├── index.ts
├── game/
│   ├── GameManager.ts
│   ├── agent.ts
│   ├── executor.ts
│   ├── competitors.ts
│   └── ids.ts
├── routes/
│   ├── auth.ts
│   ├── battle.ts
│   ├── challenges.ts
│   └── state.ts
├── shared/
│   └── types.ts
└── test/
    ├── GameManager.test.ts

````

### User (2026-05-19T17:17:09.712Z)

Does the plan use vercel ai sdk?

### Assistant [final_answer] (2026-05-19T17:17:13.838Z)

Yes. The plan explicitly uses the Vercel AI SDK in the **Agent Runner** section:

```ts
// backend/game/agent.ts
Use Vercel AI SDK generateText.
```

It specifies:

- `agent.ts` owns LLM interaction
- uses `generateText`
- includes competitor `strategy` in the system prompt
- registers `executeCode` as the tool
- propagates `AbortSignal`
- emits runtime events like started, tool call, answer submitted, timeout/error

So the intended flow is:

```txt
GameManager starts skirmish
 -> agent.ts runs each competitor with Vercel AI SDK generateText
 -> generateText can call executeCode tool
 -> executor.ts runs generated code in sandbox
 -> agent.ts returns final answer
 -> GameManager resolves eliminations
```

### User (2026-05-19T17:18:47.554Z)

how are competitors created and stored?

### Assistant [final_answer] (2026-05-19T17:18:56.341Z)

In the plan, competitors are created when the admin starts a battle.

Flow:

1. Admin calls `POST /battle/configure` with `competitorCount`.
2. Admin calls `POST /battle/start`.
3. `GameManager.startBattle()` generates that many competitors.
4. The competitors are stored in memory inside the current `Battle` object:

```ts
type Battle = {
  id: string;
  status: BattleStatus;
  configuredCompetitorCount: number;
  competitors: Competitor[];
  skirmishHistory: SkirmishSummary[];
  winner: CompetitorPublic | null;
  ...
};
```

Each competitor looks like:

```ts
type Competitor = {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  strategy: string;
  status: "active" | "competing" | "eliminated";
  eliminatedAt: string | null;
};
```

So storage is:

```ts
GameManager
  -> battle
    -> competitors[]
```

They are **not persisted** to a database in v1. If the backend process restarts, the battle and competitors are lost.

Competitors should probably be generated from a fixed local bank of names and strategies, something like:

```ts
[
  {
    name: "Stacksmith",
    strategy: "Prefer writing small scripts and verifying outputs before answering."
  },
  {
    name: "Byte Oracle",
    strategy: "Reason mathematically first, then use code only when needed."
  }
]
```

If there are more competitors than predefined personalities, generate numbered variants.

````text
### Core Design

`GameManager` owns all mutable live game state:

```ts
class GameManager {
  battle: Battle | null;
  activeSkirmish: Skirmish | null;
  pendingChallenges: Challenge[];
  spectators: Set<ServerWebSocket>;
}
```

HTTP routes validate requests and call `GameManager` methods. They should not directly mutate battle, competitor, challenge, or skirmish state.

Agent execution and code execution stay behind narrow functional interfaces:

```ts
runAgentForChallenge(input): Promise<AgentResult>
executeCode(input, signal): Promise<ExecutionResult>
```

## Public Types

Define public frontend-safe types in `backend/shared/types.ts`.

### Battle

```ts
type BattleStatus = "configuring" | "active" | "finished";

type Battle = {
  id: string;
  status: BattleStatus;
  configuredCompetitorCount: number;
  competitors: Competitor[];
  skirmishHistory: SkirmishSummary[];
  winner: CompetitorPublic | null;
  createdAt: string;
  startedAt: string | null;
  finishedAt: string | null;
};
```

### Competitor

```ts
type CompetitorStatus = "active" | "competing" | "eliminated";

type Competitor = {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  strategy: string;
  status: CompetitorStatus;
  eliminatedAt: string | null;
};
```

`strategy` is used in the competitor system prompt to create visible variation between agents. Keep it simple for v1: a short prompt fragment selected from a fixed bank.

### Challenge

```ts
type Challenge = {
  id: string;
  prompt: string;
  expectedAnswer: string;
  submittedBy: string;
  status: "queued" | "running" | "completed" | "canceled";
  createdAt: string;
};
```

Important: `expectedAnswer` is never sent to spectators or agents.

Frontend-facing challenge DTO:

```ts
type ChallengePublic = Omit<Challenge, "expectedAnswer">;
```

### Skirmish

```ts
type Skirmish = {
  id: string;
  battleId: string;
  challenge: Challenge;
  competitorIds: string[];
  status: "running" | "resolved" | "canceled";
  startedAt: string;
  resolvedAt: string | null;
  results: SkirmishCompetitorResult[];
};
```


````

### Tool Output: call_Tp7ee45RCTi4VEPMTptgmMdi (2026-05-19T17:20:24.936Z)

````text
Chunk ID: 3daf0d
Wall time: 0.0000 seconds
Process exited with code 0
Original token count: 558
Output:
  seq: number;
};
```

`seq` is a monotonically increasing event counter used by clients to detect missed live updates.

## HTTP API

### Public Routes

```txt
GET  /game/state
POST /challenges/submit
POST /challenges/enqueue
GET  /ws
```

For MVP, `/challenges/submit` and `/challenges/enqueue` can share the same queue behavior:

- If a battle is active, the challenge enters the active pending queue.
- If no battle is active, the challenge remains queued for the next battle.
- The frontend can present these differently, but the backend can store them in the same queue initially.

`POST /challenges/submit` body:

```ts
{
  username: string;
  prompt: string;
  expectedAnswer: string;
}
```

Validation:

- `username`: required, trimmed, max 40 characters.
- `prompt`: required, trimmed, max 2,000 characters.
- `expectedAnswer`: required, trimmed, max 500 characters.
- Reject empty strings after trim.

Response:

```ts
{
  challenge: ChallengePublic;
  state: GameState;
}
```

### Admin Routes

```txt
POST   /auth/login
POST   /battle/configure
POST   /battle/start
DELETE /challenges/queue
DELETE /challenges/:id
```

`POST /auth/login`:

- Accept one admin password from `ADMIN_PASSWORD`.
- Return a signed JWT or signed opaque token.
- Use `Authorization: Bearer <token>` for admin routes.
- A single admin account is sufficient.

`POST /battle/configure` body:

```ts
{
  competitorCount: number;
}
```

Validation:

- Only allowed when no active battle exists or current battle is `"configuring"` / `"finished"`.
- Recommended competitor count range: 4 to 32.
- Store the configuration but do not start the battle.

`POST /battle/start`:

- Creates a new battle if none exists or if the previous battle is finished.
- Generates competitors from the configured count.
- Sets battle status to `"active"`.
- Calls `maybeStartNextSkirmish()` after starting.

`DELETE /challenges/queue`:

- Admin clears all pending challenges.
- Active skirmish is not affected.

`DELETE /challenges/:id`:

- Admin deletes one queued challenge if it is still pending.
- If the challenge is already running or completed, return a 409 conflict.

## WebSocket Events

All server-to-client events include `seq` and `state`-safe payloads.


````

### Tool Output: call_3MwR5GvfQcbp2rx9scpCrQ3P (2026-05-19T17:20:24.937Z)

````text
Chunk ID: 2f75db
Wall time: 0.0000 seconds
Process exited with code 0
Original token count: 472
Output:
- Broadcast `skirmish:canceled`.
- Do not eliminate anyone.
- Continue to the next queued challenge.

This interprets "all competitors in a skirmish are eliminated" as only the participants in that skirmish, not every competitor in the battle.

## Agent Runner

`agent.ts` owns LLM interaction.

Input:

```ts
type RunAgentInput = {
  competitor: Competitor;
  challengePrompt: string;
  signal: AbortSignal;
  onEvent: (event: AgentRuntimeEvent) => void;
};
```

Output:

```ts
type AgentResult = {
  competitorId: string;
  answer: string | null;
  elapsedMs: number;
  error: string | null;
};
```

Behavior:

- Use Vercel AI SDK `generateText`.
- Provide a system prompt that includes the competitor strategy.
- Provide the challenge prompt.
- Register one tool: `executeCode`.
- The agent must return a final answer string.
- Trim the answer before comparison.
- Propagate `AbortSignal` to model call and code execution.
- Emit runtime events for:
  - started
  - tool call
  - answer submitted
  - timeout/error

Do not include `expectedAnswer` anywhere in the agent prompt, tool input, logs sent to clients, or WebSocket payloads.

## Code Executor

`executor.ts` is the highest-risk component and should stay narrow.

Preferred MVP sandbox:

- Run generated code through Deno with restrictive permissions.
- Use no network, no filesystem, no environment, no subprocess, no FFI.
- Add a wall-clock timeout.
- Limit stdout/stderr size.
- Return only text output and structured error metadata.

Example execution policy:

```txt
deno run
  --no-net
  --no-read
  --no-write
  --no-env
  --no-ffi
  --no-run
```

Executor input:

```ts
type ExecuteCodeInput = {
  code: string;
  language: "typescript" | "javascript";
  signal: AbortSignal;
};
```

Executor output:

```ts
type ExecutionResult = {
  stdout: string;
  stderr: string;
  exitCode: number | null;
  timedOut: boolean;

````
